title: Tinted lip balm
subtitle: A beeswax lip balm with sheer colour from cosmetic mica and a peppermint tingle
excerpt: Beeswax firms the balm enough to apply from a tube or tin. Shea butter and castor oil give slip and sheen. A small amount of cosmetic mica in a rose or berry shade adds a sheer wash of colour. Peppermint essential oil is included at a lip-safe level for a mild cooling tingle.
sourceNotes: Lip balm formulation from Gracefruit cosmetic-making guide (gracefruit.com, May 2026). Cosmetic mica usage in lip products from supplier datasheet. Peppermint EO lip-safe limits from Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014).

recipe.yieldDescription: 6 lip balm tubes or 2 small tins (approximately 50 g total)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Pour into tubes or small tins. The mica must be fully dispersed in the castor oil before adding to the wax: sift through and grind any clumps with the back of a spoon.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Lip balms keep for 12 months and make excellent small gifts. Pour into clean, unused tubes or tins.
recipe.temperatureNote: Melt beeswax and shea butter over a water bath to 70 degrees Celsius. Remove from heat before adding castor oil with dispersed mica and the essential oil. Pour into tubes promptly: the blend thickens quickly as it cools.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months in a cool, dark place. Beeswax and shea butter have long shelf lives. The castor oil is the limiting factor. Discard if the balm develops a rancid smell. No water content means no preservative is required.

recipeTools.notes: Or any small heat-proof jug with a pour spout; dedicated to cosmetic use

glossaryTerms.definition: A thick, viscous carrier oil that adds shine and slip to lip products. Castor oil is the traditional gloss ingredient in lipstick and lip balm: it adheres well to the lips and resists melting at body temperature better than lighter oils.

content.text: Castor oil is the key ingredient for lip shine. It is viscous enough to coat the lips with a glossy film and to suspend mica particles without them settling to the bottom of the formula. Disperse the mica in the castor oil before adding it to the melted wax: if it goes in dry, the particles clump and create a streaky rather than even colour.
is cold-pressed and distinct from the industrial grade: use only cosmetic or food-grade.

Peppermint essential oil is included at 0.5%: the maximum recommended for a leave-on lip product by the IFRA guidelines. The cooling tingle from menthol at this level is mild. For children's lip balms, omit the peppermint entirely and substitute with a drop of vanilla oleoresin if available.

items.prepNote: Adds gloss and suspends the mica
3% of formula; rose or berry shade; lip-safe cosmetic-grade only; disperse in the castor oil before adding
0.5% of formula; lip-safe maximum; approximately 5 drops; omit for children's version

content.text: Disperse the cosmetic mica in the castor oil by stirring vigorously until fully blended: this can take 2 to 3 minutes. Set aside.

Melt the beeswax and shea butter in the pouring jug over a simmering-water bath until fully liquid. Remove from heat. Stir in the castor oil-mica blend. Allow to cool to approximately 45 degrees Celsius, then stir in the peppermint essential oil if using. Pour immediately into lip balm tubes or small tins. Leave undisturbed until fully set (around 30 minutes). Cap and label.
12 months in a cool, dark place. The balm will soften in warm conditions and firm up in cold. Store away from direct sunlight to prevent the mica colour from fading.
